derivation: Direct addressing = the universe of keys is small enough that a table slot is allocated for every possible key (CLRS direct-address table T[0..|U|-1]); B describes an ordinary hash table.
Key k maps to slot k directly, so search is a single array index: O(1) worst case.
A hash function h maps a key to an index in the table, i.e. it computes the key's location in the array.
Randomised/uniform hash functions reduce collisions and chaining resolves them, so all three listed techniques are among the mentioned methods: 'All of the mentioned'.
Load factor alpha = n/m = average number of elements per slot = average chain length under chaining.
Simple uniform hashing assumption: any element is equally likely to hash into any of the m slots, independently of the others.
Under simple uniform hashing with chaining, expected search time is Theta(1 + alpha); with alpha treated as constant this is O(1).
CLRS chains are doubly linked so that CHAINED-HASH-DELETE runs in O(1) given a pointer to the element; a singly linked list would need an O(n) predecessor search.
The E-R diagram is the graphical notation for the logical structure (schema) of a database.

A relationship set is drawn as a diamond (double diamond is reserved for the identifying relationship of a weak entity set). Shipped explanation text about dashed lines is mismatched but the option is right.
Silberschatz notation: an entity set is a rectangle split into two parts, name on top and attributes below.
An arrow from the relationship diamond to an entity set means 'at most one'; arrows to BOTH sides means at most one on each side, i.e. one-to-one.

Role labels sit on the line joining the relationship set (diamond) to the entity set (rectangle); the textbook phrasing is diamond-to-rectangle, though the line is undirected so option B describes the same edge (source-key convention picks D).
By definition, an entity set lacking attributes sufficient for a primary key is a weak entity set.

Silberschatz: the weak entity set is existence dependent on the 'identifying entity set', also called the 'owner entity set' - so options A and B are both textbook-correct names; source key and the standard phrasing of the sentence ('called the identifying entity set') select A.
